content.body: Light travels in straight lines called rays. When light hits a surface, it bounces back — this is called reflection. Mirrors use reflection to create images. Understanding reflection is key to designing cameras, telescopes, and periscopes.

Laws of Reflection:
1. The angle of incidence = the angle of reflection
   (i = r)
2. The incident ray, reflected ray, and normal all lie in the same plane

All angles are measured from the NORMAL (a line perpendicular to the surface at the point of incidence)

content.title: Plane Mirror Images
content.body:
Properties of images in a plane (flat) mirror:
• Same size as the object
• Same distance behind mirror as object is in front
• Upright (not inverted)
• Laterally inverted (left-right reversed)
• Virtual (cannot be projected on a screen)

content.title: Types of Mirrors
content.body:
Concave (converging) mirror: curves inward (like a bowl)
• Reflects parallel rays to a focal point F
• Used in: torch reflectors, shaving mirrors, car headlights, satellite dishes

Convex (diverging) mirror: curves outward
• Reflects parallel rays outward — always produces small, virtual, upright image
• Used in: rear-view mirrors, shop security mirrors, road junctions

questions.question: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. This is:
questions.explanation: The first law of reflection states that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

questions.question: Angles of incidence and reflection are measured from:
questions.explanation: All angles in reflection (and refraction) are measured from the normal — a line perpendicular to the surface.

questions.question: An image in a plane mirror is:
questions.explanation: Plane mirror images are virtual (can't be projected), the same size, upright, and laterally inverted (left-right swapped).

questions.question: Object is 8cm from a plane mirror. Image is how far from mirror?
questions.explanation: In a plane mirror, image distance = object distance. Image is 8cm behind the mirror.

questions.question: Concave mirrors are used in car headlights because:
options: They produce a wide field of view
They reflect light into a parallel beam for distance
questions.answer: They reflect light into a parallel beam for distance
questions.explanation: A light source at the focal point of a concave mirror produces a parallel beam of light — useful for headlights.

questions.question: Why are convex mirrors used as rear-view mirrors?
options: They give a wide field of view
questions.answer: They give a wide field of view
questions.explanation: Convex mirrors diverge light, creating smaller images but covering a wider area — giving drivers more visibility behind them.

questions.question: Angle of incidence = 35°. Find angle of reflection.
questions.explanation: By the law of reflection, angle of reflection = angle of incidence = 35°.

questions.question: Light travels in:
questions.explanation: Light travels in straight lines — this is why shadows have sharp edges and we can draw ray diagrams.

questions.question: A periscope uses:
questions.explanation: A periscope uses two plane mirrors at 45° to reflect light around corners, allowing you to see over obstacles.

questions.question: Which mirror always produces a virtual, upright, diminished image?
questions.explanation: Convex (diverging) mirrors always produce virtual, upright, and smaller (diminished) images, regardless of object distance.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another (e.g. from air into glass). This happens because light changes speed in different media. Refraction explains why swimming pools look shallower than they are, and why lenses can focus light.

Snell's Law:
n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂

Where:
n₁, n₂ = refractive indices of the two media
θ₁ = angle of incidence
θ₂ = angle of refraction

Refractive index n = speed of light in vacuum / speed in medium = c/v
Air/vacuum: n ≈ 1
Water: n ≈ 1.33
Glass: n ≈ 1.5

content.title: Refraction Rules
content.body:
Light bending rules:
• Going from less dense to more dense medium (air→glass): bends TOWARD the normal (angle decreases)
• Going from more dense to less dense (glass→air): bends AWAY from normal (angle increases)

At 0° incidence (perpendicular to surface): NO bending occurs

Total Internal Reflection (TIR):
When light travels from dense to less dense medium and hits at angle GREATER than critical angle θc:
sin θc = n₂/n₁ = 1/n (if going from medium to air)

For glass: θc ≈ 42°
For water: θc ≈ 49°

Applications: optical fibres (internet cables!), endoscopes, diamonds (sparkle!), bicycle reflectors

questions.question: Refraction occurs because:
options: Light is absorbed at the surface
Light changes speed in different media
questions.answer: Light changes speed in different media
questions.explanation: Refraction is caused by the change in speed of light as it moves from one medium to another.

questions.question: Light goes from air into glass. It bends:
questions.explanation: Going from less dense (air) to more dense (glass), light slows and bends toward the normal.

questions.question: The refractive index of a vacuum is:
questions.explanation: By definition, the refractive index of a vacuum (and approximately air) is 1.

questions.question: What is total internal reflection?
options: All light reflects in all media
Light is completely reflected when it hits a surface from a dense medium at > critical angle
questions.answer: Light is completely reflected when it hits a surface from a dense medium at > critical angle
questions.explanation: TIR occurs when light in a dense medium hits a less dense boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle — all light reflects back.

questions.question: Optical fibres use TIR to:
options: Transmit light signals around bends without loss
questions.answer: Transmit light signals around bends without loss
questions.explanation: Optical fibres use total internal reflection to carry light/data signals with minimal loss, even around corners.

questions.question: A swimming pool appears shallower than it is because:
options: Light refracts away from normal as it exits water
questions.answer: Light refracts away from normal as it exits water
questions.explanation: Light bends away from the normal going from water (dense) to air (less dense), making the bottom appear closer than it is.

questions.question: Snell's Law is: n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂. If n₁=1, n₂=1.5, θ₁=30°. Find sinθ₂.
questions.explanation: n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂ → 1×sin30° = 1.5×sinθ₂ → sinθ₂ = 0.5/1.5 = 0.33.

questions.question: A diamond sparkles brightly because:
options: It has a low critical angle and undergoes TIR repeatedly
questions.answer: It has a low critical angle and undergoes TIR repeatedly
questions.explanation: Diamond has a very high refractive index and low critical angle. Light entering undergoes multiple total internal reflections, creating the sparkle.

questions.question: When light travels perpendicular to a surface (angle of incidence = 0°):
options: It passes straight through without bending
questions.answer: It passes straight through without bending
questions.explanation: When light hits a surface at 90° (0° from the normal), no refraction occurs — it passes straight through.

questions.question: An endoscope used by doctors uses:
questions.explanation: Medical endoscopes use bundles of optical fibres — light travels through using TIR — allowing doctors to see inside the body.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another due to a change in speed. Light slows down in denser media.

Snell's Law:
n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂
n = refractive index, θ = angle to normal

Refractive index: n = c/v = sin(incident angle)/sin(refracted angle)
c = speed of light in vacuum, v = speed in medium

Denser medium → higher n → light bends towards normal

Total Internal Reflection (TIR):
Occurs when light travels from dense to less dense medium and angle > critical angle
Critical angle: sin(c) = 1/n

Applications:
• Optical fibres (communications and medical endoscopes)
• Diamonds (sparkle)
• Prisms in binoculars/periscopes

content.title: Optical Fibres
content.body:
Light enters at one end, hits sides at angle > critical angle → TIR → reflects along fibre → emerges at other end
Benefits: no signal loss, immune to electromagnetic interference, carry huge data
Used in: internet cables, medical imaging, telephone cables

questions.question: Refraction occurs because light...
options: Changes speed when entering a new medium
questions.answer: Changes speed when entering a new medium
questions.explanation: Light travels at different speeds in different materials. This speed change causes the direction to change (refraction).

questions.question: When light enters a denser medium, it bends...
questions.explanation: Dense medium has higher n, lower speed. By Snell's law, the angle decreases → bends towards normal.

questions.question: Snell's Law is...
questions.explanation: Snell's law: n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂ relates angles of incidence and refraction to refractive indices.

questions.question: Refractive index of a medium equals...
options: Speed in vacuum / speed in medium
Speed in medium / speed in vacuum
questions.answer: Speed in vacuum / speed in medium
questions.explanation: n = c/v. Higher n means light travels more slowly in that medium.

questions.question: Total internal reflection requires...
options: Light going from less dense to denser medium
Light going from denser to less dense medium at angle > critical angle
questions.answer: Light going from denser to less dense medium at angle > critical angle
questions.explanation: TIR only occurs when: (1) light travels from dense to less dense medium AND (2) angle of incidence exceeds critical angle.

questions.question: Critical angle is related to refractive index by...
questions.explanation: At the critical angle, refracted ray travels along surface (θ₂=90°). Snell's law → sin(c) = n₂/n₁ = 1/n (for air boundary).

questions.question: Optical fibres use TIR to...
options: Transmit data as light signals over long distances
questions.answer: Transmit data as light signals over long distances
questions.explanation: Light bounces along fibre by repeated TIR. This transmits data at the speed of light with minimal loss.

questions.question: A diamond sparkles because...
options: Its high refractive index causes TIR inside the gem
questions.answer: Its high refractive index causes TIR inside the gem
questions.explanation: Diamond has very high n (2.42) and small critical angle. Most light undergoes TIR internally, creating sparkle.

questions.question: Glass has n=1.5. Speed of light in glass (c=3×10⁸ m/s) =
In an endoscope, optical fibres allow doctors to...
options: See inside the body using light transmitted through fibres
questions.answer: See inside the body using light transmitted through fibres
questions.explanation: Medical endoscopes use optical fibres — one bundle carries light in, another carries the image out using TIR.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

lessons.title: Colour, Dispersion and the Electromagnetic Spectrum

content.body: White light is a mixture of all colours. A prism splits white light into a spectrum by dispersion. The electromagnetic spectrum is the complete range of electromagnetic waves.

Dispersion:
Different colours have different wavelengths → different speeds in glass → different refraction angles
ROY G BIV: Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Blue, Indigo, Violet
Red: longest wavelength, least refracted
Violet: shortest wavelength, most refracted

content.title: The Electromagnetic Spectrum (longest to shortest wavelength)
content.body:
Radio waves → Microwaves → Infrared → Visible light → Ultraviolet → X-rays → Gamma rays

All EM waves:
• Travel at speed of light (3×10⁸ m/s) in vacuum
• Are transverse waves
• Can travel through vacuum
• Carry energy

content.title: Uses of EM Waves
content.body:
Radio: broadcasting, communication
Microwave: cooking, satellite communication, mobile phones
Infrared: remote controls, thermal imaging, heating
Visible: sight, photography
UV: sterilisation, tanning, detecting fake notes
X-rays: medical imaging, security scanning
Gamma: cancer treatment, sterilising medical equipment

questions.question: White light is...
options: A mixture of all colours of the spectrum
questions.answer: A mixture of all colours of the spectrum
questions.explanation: White light is a combination of all visible wavelengths. A prism separates them by dispersion.

questions.question: Which colour is refracted most by a prism?
questions.explanation: Violet has shortest wavelength, travels slowest in glass → greatest change in speed → most refraction.

questions.question: All electromagnetic waves travel at...
questions.explanation: All electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light (3×10⁸ m/s) in vacuum, regardless of wavelength.

questions.question: Which EM waves have the longest wavelength?
questions.explanation: Radio waves have the longest wavelengths (up to kilometres). Gamma rays have the shortest.

questions.question: Microwave ovens work because...
options: Microwaves are absorbed by water molecules
questions.answer: Microwaves are absorbed by water molecules, heating food
questions.explanation: Microwave frequency matches resonant frequency of water molecules, causing them to vibrate rapidly → heat.

questions.question: X-rays are used in medicine to...
questions.explanation: X-rays penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone → create shadow images on detectors.

questions.question: Gamma rays are used to treat cancer by...
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are highly ionising. Focused on a tumour, they damage DNA in cancer cells, killing them.

questions.question: UV radiation from the Sun is dangerous because...
options: It can damage DNA and cause skin cancer
questions.answer: It can damage DNA and cause skin cancer
questions.explanation: UV radiation has enough energy to damage DNA, leading to mutations and potentially skin cancer.

questions.question: Infrared radiation is associated with...
questions.explanation: All warm objects emit infrared radiation. It is detected as heat and used in thermal cameras and remote controls.

questions.question: EM waves are transverse waves because...
options: The oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of travel
questions.answer: The oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of travel
questions.explanation: In transverse waves, the oscillation is at 90° to the direction of wave propagation.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Light & Optics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Light & Optics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Light & Optics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Light & Optics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Light & Optics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Light & Optics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Light & Optics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
